_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Greene County, TN. Formed 1783 from Washington County. Seat: Greeneville — Andrew Johnson's home town, future president and the county's most famous resident. Unionist stronghold during the Civil War. The Nolichucky River runs through the county's heart. Broad agricultural valley flanked by the Bald Mountains to the south and the Great Valley ridges to the north.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Great Appalachian Valley (Valley and Ridge Province). Greene County sits in the central portion of the East Tennessee Great Valley, between the Bald Mountains (Unaka Range) to the southeast and the parallel ridges of the Valley and Ridge system to the northwest.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 1,200–4,800 ft. The valley floor around Greeneville sits at approximately 1,550 ft. The Bald Mountains on the county's southeastern border reach 4,800 ft.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Broad limestone valley along the Nolichucky River, with rolling farmland on either side. The county's southeastern quarter climbs into the Bald Mountains — rugged, forested, and sparsely settled. The valley floor is the agricultural core: wide, well-drained, and productive. The contrast between the fertile valley and the mountain backcountry defined the county's social geography — valley families were more prosperous and politically connected; mountain families were more isolated and self-sufficient.

soil_types.description: Deep, well-drained limestone residual soil on the valley floor. The county's most productive agricultural soil.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Sustained productive farming across the entire era range. Corn, wheat, and tobacco (after 1870) grew well on these soils.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, TN (1956).

soil_types.description: Red residual soil on shale bedrock, found on the transitional slopes between valley floor and mountain base.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: More erosion-prone than the limestone soils. Farms on Tellico soils were smaller and required more careful management. Erosion was a problem after the Civil War when overworked farms lost topsoil.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, TN (1956).

soil_types.description: Very shallow, rocky soil on steep mountain slopes in the Bald Mountains. Forest soil unsuitable for cultivation.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Mountain-slope farms in Greene County were garden-scale subsistence operations on tiny patches of deeper soil. Most of the mountain terrain could not be cultivated at all.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, TN (1956).

major_waterways.name: Nolichucky River
major_waterways.role: Primary drainage, valley corridor, mill power, flooding risk
major_waterways.description: The Nolichucky enters Greene County from the Bald Mountains through a series of gorges and then opens onto the valley floor, flowing northwest through the county's agricultural heartland. The river powered grist mills and saw mills at multiple locations. Periodic flooding enriched bottomland but destroyed crops and property.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Nolichucky built Greene County's farms with the silt it deposited and destroyed them with the floods it brought — {ancestor_name} lived on bottomland that was the best soil in the county and the most dangerous.

major_waterways.name: Lick Creek
major_waterways.role: Greeneville water supply, local mill sites
major_waterways.description: Lick Creek flows through Greeneville and provided the town's water supply and mill power. The creek's valley is narrow and was the site of the town's earliest settlement.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: Greeneville grew where Lick Creek offered water, power, and a flat place to build — and {ancestor_name} knew the town from the courthouse, the general store, and the creek that ran behind both.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Open valley savanna and canebrakes maintained by Cherokee burning; dense hardwood and chestnut forest on Bald Mountains
vegetation_eras.description: The valley floor was largely open when European settlers arrived, with patches of canebrake and scattered trees. The Bald Mountains were densely forested — American chestnut, white oak, red oak, tulip poplar. The treeless mountain 'balds' that give the range its name were a natural feature maintained by fire, wind, and possibly grazing.
vegetation_eras.source: Davis, Where There Are Mountains, ch. 2.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Cleared farmland on valley floor; orchards and pasture on hillsides; undisturbed forest on mountain slopes
vegetation_eras.description: Settlement cleared the valley systematically. Split-rail fences divided farms. Apple orchards became a Greene County specialty — the elevation and climate suited them. The mountain backcountry remained forested and thinly settled.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Mixed farmland and tobacco fields; chestnut blight on mountains; Greeneville growing as market town
vegetation_eras.description: Tobacco cultivation expanded after the Civil War and became the dominant cash crop by 1900. The burley tobacco curing barns became a defining feature of the valley landscape. Chestnut blight killed the mountain forest's dominant tree. Greeneville grew slowly but steadily as a market and governmental center.
vegetation_eras.source: Trefousse, Hans L. Andrew Johnson: A Biography. New York: W.W. Norton, 1989.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The Nolichucky valley in October was golden — corn shocks standing in rows across the bottomland, the Bald Mountains beyond them blazing with the reds and oranges of oak and hickory and the deep brown of dying chestnut leaves. The air was cool and dry, smelling of cut corn and chimney smoke. From the fields, the sound was stalks being stacked, wagons creaking on the road to the mill, children shouting as they chased hogs out of the corn. The Nolichucky ran low and clear in autumn, and the limestone-bottomed fords were easy. Greeneville's courthouse bell could be heard a mile away when the wind was right — a sound that marked the center of a farming county that fed itself well.

Greene County in the winter of 1863 was a place where a man could be hanged for his politics. Unionist families hid in the Bald Mountains; Confederate patrols worked the valley roads. Greeneville itself changed hands multiple times. The night was cold — 20 degrees and dropping — and the farmhouses sat dark, no light showing that might attract the wrong patrol. The smell was frost and fear. The sound was silence, broken by the occasional distant crack of a rifle or the bark of a dog that had heard something its owner hoped was a deer. Andrew Johnson's house stood empty, its owner in Washington working for the Union. The valley that had always been a place of stability was now a place where the wrong word at the wrong store could end a family.

The tobacco fields ran in neat green rows down the valley, and by July the plants were waist-high and sticky with tar — the smell of burley tobacco was the smell of Greene County in summer, sweet and pungent and inescapable. The curing barns dotted the hillsides, their vertical-slatted walls designed to let air pass through the hanging leaf. Heat built through the morning — 85 degrees by noon, humid, with the thunderheads piling up over the Bald Mountains. The Nolichucky ran sluggish and warm. In Greeneville, the courthouse square had automobiles parked where horses had stood, but the rhythm of the tobacco crop still governed every conversation and every loan at the bank.

landscape_changes.description: Burley tobacco replaced cotton and grain as the primary cash crop after the Civil War. Tobacco curing barns — distinctive vertical-slatted wooden structures — became the defining architectural feature of the agricultural landscape. The tobacco economy changed the labor calendar and created a new relationship with national markets through the tobacco warehouses in Greeneville.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Post-Civil War ancestor occupations in Greene County should be interpreted through the tobacco economy. A farmer's prosperity depended on tobacco prices set in distant markets.

landscape_changes.change: Civil War devastation of the community fabric
landscape_changes.description: Greene County was a Unionist stronghold in a Confederate state. The Civil War split families, neighbors, and churches. Bushwhacking, bridge burning (November 1861), and reprisal executions tore the community apart. The execution of bridge burners in Greeneville was one of the most notorious incidents of the East Tennessee war.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Civil War records in Greene County must be read through the lens of divided loyalty. A man's military service — or lack of it — was a political statement that could cost his family everything.
landscape_changes.source: Fisher, Noel C. War at Every Door: Partisan Politics and Guerrilla Violence in East Tennessee, 1860-1869. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1997.

narrative_hooks: Greene County was Andrew Johnson's county — the tailor who became president, the Unionist from the Confederate state. {ancestor_name} lived in Johnson's orbit whether {pronoun_subject} voted for him or not.
The Nolichucky valley gave {ancestor_name} the best farmland in the mountains — flat, fertile, and forgiving — but the river that made the bottomland could take it back in a single flood.

In the Civil War, Greene County was Unionist ground — and {ancestor_name} either stood with the Union, served the Confederacy, or tried to be invisible, because there was no neutral ground in East Tennessee.
By {year}, the tobacco barns on the hillsides told the story of Greene County's economy as clearly as the corn shocks had a generation earlier — the valley grew what the market would pay for.
The Bald Mountains above Greene County were a different world from the valley floor — isolated, forested, and home to families who lived closer to the frontier than to the courthouse.
source_references: Finger, John R. Tennessee Frontiers: Three Regions in Transition. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2001.
Fisher, Noel C. War at Every Door: Partisan Politics and Guerrilla Violence in East Tennessee, 1860-1869. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1997.
Trefousse, Hans L. Andrew Johnson: A Biography. New York: W.W. Norton, 1989.
Davis, Donald Edward. Where There Are Mountains: An Environmental History of the Southern Appalachians. Athens: University of Georgia Press, 2000.
USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, TN (1956).
